planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before transplanting; leeks are slow-growing and need a long season. Transplant outdoors 4–6 weeks before last frost. In zone 9, start seeds in late summer for a fall-through-spring growing season.

directions: Start seeds indoors and transplant to fertile, well-drained, deeply worked, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; leeks are one of the most versatile and cold-hardy of all cultivated alliums, producing thick, blanched white shafts with mild, sweet onion flavor used extensively in European cooking — particularly French, Belgian, and British cuisines. The edible part is the blanched white to pale green shaft below the light-excluding soil or mulch; mounding soil around the stems ('hilling up') promotes longer, more tender white portions. Unlike onions that form bulbs, leeks grow as extended cylindrical shafts without bulbing. In zone 9a, leeks are best grown as a fall-through-spring crop — planted in late summer and harvested from December through spring. They tolerate hard frost and brief freezing without damage. 'American Flag' and 'Giant Musselburgh' are reliably good heirloom varieties.

planting.note: Plant sets (small bulbs) in fall 4–6 weeks before first frost for a winter-to-spring crop; in zone 9, fall planting (October–November) is strongly preferred over spring planting. Plant sets 1 inch deep, pointed end up, in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun.

directions: Plant sets in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; shallots are prized in gourmet cuisine for their sweeter, more complex flavor than onions — a sophisticated blend of onion and mild garlic notes that makes them the preferred choice in French cooking for vinaigrettes, béarnaise, and fine sauces. Each planted set produces a cluster of 4–12 new bulbs, making them productive and economical. The bulbs store well at room temperature for 6+ months when properly dried. In zone 9a, fall planting allows bulbs to establish through the mild Gulf Coast winter and produce mature, harvestable shallots by April–May before summer heat arrives. French Grey Shallot ('Grise de Bretagne') is the premium culinary shallot; 'Dutch Yellow' and 'Prisma' are more readily available varieties with excellent yield.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; violet mustard is a cool-season biennial that germinates in fall, overwinters, and produces edible leaves through winter and beautiful violet flowers in early spring before setting seed.

directions: Direct sow in fall in moderately fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; violet mustard is a Chinese brassica grown both as a winter vegetable and as a spring ornamental — the young leaves through winter have a mild, pleasant flavor used fresh in salads or cooked, and the plant concludes its season with a spectacular display of pale to bright violet flowers in spring that are edible as garnish and highly attractive to early pollinators. The winter foliage often develops attractive purple-violet tints in cold weather. In zone 9, it self-sows to naturalize, returning each fall for a winter vegetable and spring flower display with no replanting. An excellent edible-ornamental for the winter garden.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall (in zone 8–10) or early spring 4–6 weeks before last frost; arugula matures quickly and should be succession sown every 2–3 weeks for continuous harvest. In zone 9, the prime season is October through April — arugula bolts rapidly in heat above 80°F.

directions: Direct sow thickly in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; arugula is one of the fastest and most rewarding salad greens for the fall and winter garden in zone 9, germinating within days and producing peppery, nutty, distinctly flavored leaves in as little as 35 days. Begin harvesting outer leaves when plants reach 3–4 inches; the more you harvest, the more it produces. The flavor intensifies as the plant ages and strengthens further in cold weather — a mild spring frost improves the flavor. Bolting to flower with summer heat renders leaves too pungent for most uses, though the flowers themselves are edible and spicy. Succession sow every 2–3 weeks from October through February in zone 9a for a continuous harvest through the cool season. 'Astro' is a particularly bolt-resistant cultivar for warm climates.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall (zone 8-10) or in spring 2 weeks before last frost; dwarf nasturtium is a compact, bushy form that does not trail. Sow directly where it is to grow — nasturtiums resent transplanting. In zone 9, fall-spring is the prime growing season.

directions: Direct sow in lean, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; dwarf nasturtium is a compact, bushy form of nasturtium that stays under 14 inches — ideal for containers, edging, and small gardens where the larger trailing species would overrun the space. All parts are edible: the peppery, watercress-flavored leaves are used in salads, the bright flowers as garnish and salad decoration, and the young seed pods pickled as a caper substitute. Unlike many edible flowers, nasturtiums are genuinely flavorful rather than merely decorative. The abundant flowers are rich in nectar and attract hummingbirds and long-tongued bumblebees. The plants prefer lean soil — rich, highly fertilized soils produce lush leaves at the expense of flowers. In zone 9a, grow as a winter annual sown in October for winter and spring display.
